title: OmniFocus vs Things 3 for Power users
meta_description: See why Things 3 fails first when contexts and why OmniFocus is the better pick for power users.
constraint_lens: You need a task manager that supports deep GTD workflows with contexts, perspectives, and automated review structures.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users running strict GTD workflows.
one_second_verdict.reason: Things 3 fails first because contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited.

verdict.summary: OmniFocus wins for power users running strict GTD workflows. It includes contexts, perspectives, and built-in review cycles that support structured task management. Things 3 focuses on clean project lists and areas but limits advanced filtering and automation rules. If contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited, Things 3 fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited, Things 3 fails first.

sections.heading: Why OmniFocus fits Power users better
sections.content: OmniFocus fits this power user because the filtering mechanism creates gains in several places at once. It changes how quickly the user can find the right work, how much manual sorting is needed each day, and how well the task system keeps up once lists become large or rule-heavy. That makes this a question of operational control, not just one extra filter feature.

bullets.point: OmniFocus turns large task lists into targeted working views
bullets.why_it_matters: Filters, perspectives, or query rules reduce the amount of noise you have to scan before deciding what matters now.

bullets.point: OmniFocus saves time by automating repeat organization
bullets.why_it_matters: You do not have to keep rebuilding the same views by hand when the system can apply the logic for you.

bullets.point: OmniFocus supports more deliberate control over structure
bullets.why_it_matters: Power users can shape how work appears and behaves instead of accepting one fixed list model for everything.

bullets.point: Things 3 feels calmer when the task list is still manageable by sight
bullets.why_it_matters: A simpler view can be enough if the user is not yet dealing with enough volume to justify query logic.

bullets.point: Things 3 reduces feature surface during routine use
bullets.why_it_matters: Fewer advanced controls can make the app feel lighter when power-user depth would mostly stay unused.

bullets.point: Things 3 favors direct navigation over system tuning
bullets.why_it_matters: Some users would rather visit projects and lists manually than maintain a layer of saved logic.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: OmniFocus becomes heavier than necessary when the task list is still small enough to manage by sight and the user would not maintain the extra logic.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Things 3 if direct navigation is enough and advanced rules would mostly sit unused.

items.fails_when: Things 3 breaks down when task volume grows and the user keeps doing manual sorting that a stronger filter or query system should have absorbed.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose OmniFocus when the list has outgrown simple project-by-project browsing.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the task list is still small enough that the user can manage it by sight and would not maintain more advanced rules anyway. Then Things 3 may feel simpler in the right way.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose OmniFocus if large task lists need filters, rules, or smarter views to stay usable.
Choose Things 3 if the list is still simple enough to manage without extra logic.
Avoid Things 3 when manual sorting is becoming a daily tax.

faqs.q: Why do filters and rules matter so much here?
faqs.a: Because the same logic affects findability, daily speed, and whether a large task list stays manageable without constant manual cleanup.
Things 3 is still enough when the list is small enough that browsing by eye is faster than maintaining advanced logic.

faqs.q: What is the signal that Things 3 has become too shallow?
faqs.a: It is usually when the user keeps doing repetitive manual sorting that a stronger rule system should have taken over.

faqs.q: Does OmniFocus automatically mean more complexity?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. For a power user, better logic can actually remove complexity by reducing repeated manual organization.
